topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a gavel strike. Professor Albright was leaving. My thesis advisor. The words swam before my eyes, blurring the screen until the familiar font seemed alien and menacing. My hands began to shake, a fine tremor that made gripping my lukewarm coffee cup feel like a monumental effort. I reread the message, the subject line—"Update"—echoing hollowly in the quiet of my dorm room. Every muscle in my body tightened; my breath hitched in my throat, shallow and rapid. I stared at the blinking cursor on my laptop, unable to type, unable to formulate a coherent response. A cold dread began to seep into my bones, a paralyzing chill that made the air feel thick and suffocating. I needed to call him, to ask if this was a mistake, but the phone felt impossibly heavy, glued to my palm.

[story 2]

I walked into the departmental common room, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead like trapped insects. Everyone seemed to be chatting easily, laughing about weekend plans, but I felt utterly isolated, a spotlight focused solely on my mounting anxiety. When I finally cornered a passing graduate student, my voice came out thin and reedy. "Professor Albright is leaving?" I managed, the question barely audible. The student paused, his expression shifting from casual to concerned. As he began to speak, a wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I gripped the strap of my backpack so hard my knuckles turned white. The reality of the impending void—the sudden absence of the structure that had been holding my academic world together—felt vast and utterly unmanageable. I just wanted to retreat, to disappear into the wallpaper.

[story 3]

The library felt cavernous, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming inside my chest. I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting someone to burst in and confirm the news. My focus on the footnotes of my draft disintegrated. My mind cycled through worst-case scenarios: abandonment, a complete derailment of months of painstaking work, the sudden collapse of my entire academic trajectory. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the visual confirmation of the impending change. A bead of sweat traced a cold path down my temple. The weight of responsibility, suddenly unanchored, pressed down on my shoulders, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I started pacing the narrow aisle, a restless, caged animal, desperate for an explanation that would somehow make the chaos manageable.
